Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time on weekends, but with limited resources at home and sharing devices with family, it rarely worked out as planned. I mostly studied in short bursts whenever I could grab some quiet time, maybe five hours total in a good week. I relied heavily on visual materials like diagrams and charts because they stick in my head better than reading long texts.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on deadlines. When an assignment was due, I'd suddenly be online way more, trying to catch up on everything at once. Other weeks I'd barely touch the course because I was dealing with other stuff at home or just felt too anxious to start. It wasn't ideal, but it's how I survived.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or a notification reminding me something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were any announcements I'd missed because missing important info made my anxiety worse. Occasionally I'd go in when I was bored and actually wanted something to do, but that was rare.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester where I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS every few days. The mindfulness exercises they introduced actually helped me feel calmer about logging in. But then things got hectic with family responsibilities and that routine fell apart. It never really became automatic for me.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline because I'm a visual learner and I could pause and rewatch parts I didn't understand. I also found the discussion posts helpful sometimes because seeing how other students explained concepts gave me different perspectives. Anything with diagrams or visual summaries was gold for me.
Q6: I mostly ignored the long reading materials and some of the optional practice quizzes. The readings felt endless and I'd zone out halfway through, so I'd just skim them or skip entirely. With limited time and energy, I had to prioritize what would actually help me pass, and dense text wasn't it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or understand how it connected to assignments and exams. If I watched a video and suddenly the assignment made sense, that felt valuable. But if something seemed like busywork with no clear payoff, I'd lose interest fast because I just don't have time to waste.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones I'd force myself through even when anxious because I knew they affected my grade. Optional stuff, even if interesting, got pushed aside because I was always playing catch-up. It's frustrating because sometimes the optional things probably would have helped me understand better.
Q9: Yes, definitely. I'd download videos and PDFs when I had good internet access and then study them offline at home. Sometimes I'd take screenshots of important diagrams and review them on my phone. I also made handwritten notes from the visual materials because writing helps me remember things better than just reading.
Q10: A lot of my actual learning happened when I was reviewing my handwritten notes or looking at downloaded materials offline. I'd spend time just staring at diagrams trying to understand connections, but none of that shows up anywhere. Also, I'd sometimes discuss concepts with a cousin who's in university too, and that helped clarify things.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, download it if possible, and then rewatch it multiple times offline over the next few days. My internet isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed when I could. The LMS probably thought I only watched something once when I actually reviewed it several times.
Q12: Mid-semester got really hard when my anxiety peaked. I'd stare at the LMS homepage and feel this wave of dread about everything I was behind on. Family stuff was also demanding more of my attention, and I just couldn't focus. The boredom would set in too, making everything feel pointless.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from helping out at home or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Confusion about assignments made me avoid them longer because I didn't know where to start. Sometimes I'd log in, feel overwhelmed by the workload, and just log out without doing anything.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about other things. I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything because I was too anxious to focus. It felt like going through the motions just to feel like I was doing something.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, especially the ones that confused me. The anxiety made it worse because I'd avoid things that stressed me out, even knowing the deadline was coming. It's like a cycle where avoiding it made me more anxious, which made me avoid it more. I know it's not smart but I couldn't help it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd cram everything into the last day or two before something was due. Without a deadline, I'd keep putting things off indefinitely. The LMS probably shows these spikes of activity right before due dates, and that's just how I functioned.
Q17: When I got feedback that was encouraging, it motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd feel lost about whether I was on the right track. Good feedback made me feel like the effort was worth it, bad or no feedback made me feel like why bother.
Q18: The course structure was okay but having eight online courses simultaneously was overwhelming. I needed more flexibility in deadlines given my situation. The weekly modules helped me know what to focus on, but when I fell behind, catching up felt impossible because everything kept piling up.
Q19: Having videos and visual materials made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was when instructions were unclear or when there were too many text-heavy resources. Also, the discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, like I was posting just to meet a quota rather than because I had something meaningful to say.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for answers or trying to understand what's expected without actually completing work. They could be anxious like me, clicking around trying to figure things out but feeling too overwhelmed to submit anything. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show up in my activity logs. My exam score was decent because I understood the material from my own studying methods, even if my LMS engagement looked moderate. The system doesn't capture everything.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or how much effort you put in offline. They miss the anxiety behind every login, the hours spent reviewing downloaded materials, and the real struggle to balance everything. Numbers don't tell the whole story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material, not just completing tasks to pass. It's when I'm genuinely curious and want to understand something deeply. For me, real engagement happens when I can connect concepts visually and see how they fit together, even if that happens outside the LMS.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety I battle just to open it, or how hard I work with limited resources. It wouldn't capture my offline studying, my discussions with family, or the mental energy it takes to keep going when I want to give up. My resilience doesn't show up in login counts.
